data.title: Japan still popular with Hong Kong travellers over Christmas, Harbin gaining appeal
data.description:
More Hongkongers are travelling over the Christmas holiday period compared with last year, with traditional destinations in Japan remaining popular despite geopolitical tensions, and mainland Chinese cities such as Harbin and Yunnan gaining appeal.
Some industry insiders said Japan remained popular among Hong Kong travellers despite recent news of earthquakes and a geopolitical row between Beijing and Tokyo, which earlier saw mainland visitors cancelling their trips in droves.
Relations between Japan and China cooled significantly following remarks in early November by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, who said an attack on Taiwan by the People’s Liberation Army could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” and allow Tokyo to engage in military action.
In response, Beijing issued official travel advisories urging citizens to avoid going to Japan due to security concerns, with Chinese airlines cancelling over 40 per cent of flights between the two countries in December.
Takaichi sought to ease tensions earlier this month by saying Tokyo’s position remained “unchanged” from a 1972 joint communique with China.
Outbound travel started on Christmas Eve on Wednesday, when the number of Hong Kong residents departing the airport jumped 22 per cent to 65,133 from the same day a year ago, according to the Immigration Department.
The two consecutive public holidays on Christmas Day on Thursday and Boxing Day on Friday form an extended four-day weekend.
Moon Yau Moon-yee, assistant general manager at Sunflower Travel Services and vice-chairman of the Tourism Industry Employees’ General Union, explained that, regardless of geopolitical concerns, the unique winter appeal of Japan continued to drive demand among Hongkongers.
“Hokkaido’s snow scenery, hot springs and skiing activities have a unique attraction for Hong Kong tourists, ensuring that Japan tour groups remain in short supply,” he said.
Travel platform Trip.com’s data further highlighted that travellers were diversifying their itineraries within the country, with bookings for niche destinations such as Hiroshima recording triple-digit year-on-year growth, a company spokeswoman told the Post, without specifying what the figures were.
Trip.com data also showed Hongkongers were averaging three overseas trips per year, higher than the global average of 2.4 trips, with Christmas and Easter the annual peak periods for departures.
The mainland also emerged as a major competitor for travel among Hong Kong residents, owing to the difference in the holiday calendar across the border.
“Since the mainland does not have Christmas holidays, the flow of people does not increase significantly, which has become a major reason for attracting Hong Kong people to visit during this period,” Yau said.
Destinations in northeast China were especially popular this year, with places such as Harbin particularly favoured, as the city hosted its " title="" target="_self">largest-scale Ice and Snow World festival to date.
Other regions that recorded significant growth included Yunnan, where the number of visitors tripled compared with last year, as well as Guizhou and Shandong.
For those planning shorter “flash trips” over the four-day weekend starting December 25, South Korea and Southeast Asia were the preferred choices.
Yau said the favourable exchange rate of the Korean won had boosted the appeal of cities such as Seoul and Busan.
Experience-driven travel was also becoming a defining characteristic of the Hong Kong travellers’ market.
Trip.com reported that 2026 would see a continuation of concert tourism, with spikes in bookings linked to major performance tours such as those by K-pop girl group Blackpink and solo artist G-Dragon.

This trend was complemented by a high demand for international theme parks and immersive exhibitions.
Despite the high volume of travel, concerns regarding safety and volatility remained significant among Hong Kong residents.
According to the 2025 Allianz Partners Travel Index, 53 per cent of travellers expressed anxiety about personal safety, while 52 per cent were concerned about extreme weather.
Such anxieties led to a more cautious approach to trip planning, with 90 per cent of respondents indicating that “purchasing insurance is essential for peace of mind and protection against unforeseen events”.
Fanny Yeung Shuk-fun, executive director of the Travel Industry Council, said on Wednesday that South Korea had emerged as a more popular destination than Japan this Christmas.
“The main reason is that Japan has experienced several incidents in the past one or two months, including bear attacks and earthquakes, which have caused its popularity to drop slightly,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong to broaden crypto rules with new licences for dealers and custodians
data.description:
Hong Kong will broaden its crypto rule book next year, with regulators preparing new licensing regimes for virtual asset dealers and custodians, as the city pushes to deepen its digital asset market even as Beijing tightens its scrutiny on crypto activities on the mainland.
Regulators said on Wednesday they would finalise legislative proposals for two new licensing frameworks – one for providers of virtual asset dealing services, previously referred to as over-the-counter (OTC) trading services, and another for custodian services – with the aim of introducing a bill into the Legislative Council in 2026.
Since announcing its ambition in late 2022 to become a leading digital-asset hub, Hong Kong has rolled out licensing regimes for crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers and encouraged tokenisation activities, but such initiatives have faced hurdles such as high compliance costs and heightened scrutiny.
In a government statement, SFC chief executive officer Julia Leung called the latest plans “significant progress” that would keep Hong Kong “at the global forefront” of digital asset market developments, and said that the city would maintain “an unwavering commitment to responsible innovation”.

The latest measures reflect Hong Kong’s push to deepen its digital asset market even as the sector faces growing headwinds.
A regulatory regime for virtual asset custodian services was “particularly important” because properly regulated custodians were “key for greater retail and institutional adoption of crypto” as well as Hong Kong’s global digital asset hub status, said Andrew Fei, a partner at King & Wood Mallesons in Hong Kong.
Other major jurisdictions such as the US had already been “paving the way for digital asset custodians to be regulated in a more sensible manner”, Fei noted.
Hong Kong started formulating detailed plans to regulate OTC crypto trading services last year after one of the largest financial frauds in Hong Kong’s history, which involved the allegedly fraudulent crypto exchange JPEX that resulted in roughly HK$1.6 billion (US$225 million) in losses.
OTC services facilitate direct and private large-volume cryptocurrency transactions between two parties. Physical OTC shops were found to be one of the “main avenues for channelling retail investors’ funds” into fraudulent schemes in the JPEX case, regulators said at the time.

data.title: China sharpens criticism of Japan’s ‘neo-militarism’, signals readiness to respond
data.description:
Chinese state media have escalated criticism of what they characterise as Japan’s growing “neo-militarism”, warning that Beijing has sufficient policy tools in reserve to respond should Tokyo endanger China’s core interests.
People’s Daily, a Communist Party mouthpiece, said that “neo-militarism” – the resurgence of Japanese militarism after the end of the second world war – had dealt a serious blow to bilateral relations and undermined regional peace and stability.
“Post-war Japan has failed to carry out a thorough reckoning with militarism … This ‘neo-militarism’, cloaked in the guise of a ‘peaceful nation’, is gradually becoming a real danger to Asia and the world at large,” the commentary stated on Thursday.
The remarks coincide with heightened tensions in Sino-Japanese ties, as Beijing has continued to accuse Tokyo of taking steps to expand its military footprint.


Japan’s Official Security Assistance budget, which provides security equipment aid to like-minded countries, is set to reach a record 18.1 billion yen (US$116 million) under the foreign ministry’s budget proposal for financial year 2026, released on Tuesday – more than double the amount allocated in the previous financial year.
Last week, an unnamed senior Japanese security official in the prime minister’s office reportedly told local media off the record that Tokyo should possess nuclear weapons to deter potential aggressors.
The comments sparked backlash both domestically and abroad, prompting the Japanese government to later reaffirm its decades-old pledge never to possess nuclear arms.
On Wednesday, China’s foreign ministry said the official’s remarks were not an isolated case but part of a broader effort by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi and right-wing forces in Japan to test international red lines.
Foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian said the comments exposed Tokyo’s “long-standing erroneous positions and continued push for ‘re-militarisation’, sending an extremely dangerous signal”.
In the two months since Takaichi took office, Japan has accelerated military expansion, bringing forward by two years its target of defence spending at 2 per cent of GDP, Lin said.
He added that Japan had also eased arms export restrictions, advanced revisions to key security documents and floated changes to the Three Non-Nuclear Principles in place since the 1960s, including hints at introducing nuclear-powered submarines.
Yuyuan Tantian, a social media account affiliated with Chinese state broadcaster CCTV, said in an article on Thursday that Beijing’s recent countermeasures such as sanctions and official statements showed its determination to “decisively block any possibility of a resurgence of Japanese militarism”.

Huo Zhengxin, a law professor at the China University of Political Science and Law, told Yuyuan Tantian that countermeasures targeting Japan were now at a stage of “preparation and observation” and that their direction was clear.
China had a range of policy tools in reserve, Huo said, and warned that if Beijing’s core interests were affected, the response would extend beyond diplomatic actions.
Bilateral relations have nosedived since Takaichi last month suggested an attack by Beijing on Taiwan could be seen as a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan that could trigger a military response.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
According to the People’s Daily commentary, Japan has made dangerous departures from its post-war framework across multiple domains.
It said Japan’s defence policy had rapidly shifted from an exclusively defensive posture towards a more autonomous, offensive-oriented approach, with the Self-Defence Forces now possessing “pre-emptive strike” capabilities.
It also blasted Japan’s “neo-militarism” as contradictory, arguing that the country “presents itself as a guardian of regional security while repeatedly provoking disputes”.
The commentary accused Tokyo of deliberately fanning tensions in recent years over issues such as the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea.
Furthermore, it said that by strengthening alliances and quasi-allied ties with countries including the US, Britain and Australia, it had “intentionally fuelled bloc-based confrontation in the region”.
“Takaichi has shown militarist tendencies not only in her stance on the use of force regarding Taiwan, but also in promoting historical revisionism in education,” the article added.

data.title: Ex-China table tennis player says clubs hold key to return of Wang Chuqin, Sun Yingsha
data.description:
Former China table tennis player Ma Te said he believed that Wang Chuqin’s and Sun Yingsha’s participation at the Chinese Table Tennis Super League Finals would be decided by their respective clubs.
Ma’s comments, which were shared during a livestream on his Douyin account on Wednesday, come after Wang and Sun withdrew from the WTT Finals in Hong Kong because of injury.
Discussing whether Wang would play at the Super League Finals, Ma said this depended on what Wang’s club decided, adding that players would not be fined if they did not play.
Sun’s Shenzhen University team and Wang’s Shandong Weiqiao team have both trained at the venue in Nanjing, but fans have not seen either Wang or Sun on court, leading to ongoing discussion about whether they will appear.
“Both of them are feeling somewhat better, but the degree of recovery depends on their different injuries,” Ma said. “A week is definitely too short for a full recovery.”
Wang trained at a sports centre in Beijing on Monday for the first time since withdrawing from the WTT Finals, but it is believed that he will be only a spectator at the Super League Finals.

Ma said players had been focusing on recovery and adjustments after the WTT Finals, “while a week may not seem long, for core players who have faced a packed schedule, it is already a relatively adequate period for rest”.
Ma responded to fans’ concerns about the lack of updates about the players, saying: “This is actually a good thing.
“It means they are resting well and adjusting their treatment as needed. Everyone should give athletes time to recover and wait for them to return to competition when they are ready.”
Wang withdrew from his semi-final and Sun forfeited her semi-final through injury at the WTT Finals.

data.title: How China Dai women use chickens as matchmaking tool to find husbands, reject unwanted suitors
data.description:
China’s Dai ethnic minority have an unusual way of making romantic connections.
Women make matches by taking chickens to the market during Dai New Year in the hope of attracting suitors, using the birds as matchmaking tools.
“Chicken as a Matchmaker” is the Chinese translation of a courtship custom practised by the Dai ethnic group in Xishuangbanna, Yunnan province, southwestern China.
The Dai, whose estimated population is 1.3 million, primarily live in the Xishuangbanna Dai Autonomous Prefecture and the Dehong Dai and Jingpo Autonomous Prefecture of Yunnan.

In the Dai language, the custom is called Yuesao, while the local Han people refer to it as Chuan guniang, which literally means “visiting girls”.
At the heart of the tradition is a unique romantic interaction between young men and women that takes place through the exchange of chicken meat at local markets.
During the ritual, young men seek out beautiful and kind-hearted women, while women look for hardworking and courageous men.
On the day of the Dai New Year, chickens are slaughtered and once the meat is cooked, young women dress in their finest traditional attire and take it to the market, hoping the young man they admire will come to buy it.
When cheerful young men approach and ask the price, a girl who is not interested might reply, “Eat first, then we will weigh it” and afterwards charge them double. This is regarded as a polite way of turning him down.
However, if the girl likes the young man, she will hand him a stool and invite him to sit beside her.

At that point, the young man might say: “We Dai people have a saying. Food tastes better when shared and burdens feel lighter when carried together. If we eat together, the chicken will taste even better.”
To which the girl may reply: “It tastes better when we eat freely, and it is easier when we eat freely. There is too much noise here, so let’s carry it to the woods to eat.”
The two then carry the chicken and their stools into the quiet woods where they can confess their feelings for each other.
Such romantic interactions are traditionally permitted only between October 15 and February of the following year.

They are strictly prohibited during the busy farming season, which lasts from July 15 to October 15.
Another fascinating tradition of Chuan Guniang takes place at nightfall.
Young men will silently appear, wrapped in cotton blankets with only their eyes visible, drifting quietly through the darkness.
They wander around, carefully selecting the girl they fancy, then stand beside her, hoping to strike up a conversation and make a connection.
Today, such customs have largely disappeared from rural Xishuangbanna.

data.title: Meet Bruno Gouery, who plays eccentric Frenchman Luc on Emily in Paris
data.description:
The fifth season of Emily in Paris has just come to an end, but fans still can’t get enough. The latest instalment saw our titular protagonist romance Eugenio Franceschini’s Marcello while Lucas Bravo’s Gabriel yearns for her from a distance. By the end of the season though, Emily is single again, leaving fans wondering who she’ll end up with next.

Besides Emily’s love triangle, the new season offered up some major drama with Luc, Emily’s womanising French colleague and friend at Agence Grateau. The fan-favourite character, played by Bruno Gouery, is usually the source of much-needed comic relief on the show – and season five was no exception. “With the character of Luc, I think the writers want to always … give something more funny, comical, humorous,” the actor told Tudum.
So, what do we know about Bruno Gouery?
What’s his background?

According to an interview Gouery gave to Eclair Magazine, he was born in July 1975 to a Norman chemist father and an Italian accountant mother in France. He is trilingual, speaking French, Italian and English.
Gouery spent his childhood in the suburbs of Paris and drew artistic inspiration from the “large and colourful” Italian side of his family. His first brush with comedy was through his grandfather and uncles. “It was through their contact and by observing them that I began my first performances,” he told Eclair.
Gouery studied history but did not pursue a career in that field.
How did he get into acting?

Gouery was working for a TV channel when he took a dramatics course at his co-worker’s behest, per Eclair Magazine. “It was a revelation: this is the job I want to do!” he remembered thinking.
After watching French comedy icon Michel Galabru perform on stage one day, he started taking acting lessons from him and eventually made his own stage debut alongside the legend. “[Galabru] was a role model, an inspiration, a guide whose advice and experience are still with me today,” Gouery gushed to Eclair.
Where have you seen him before?

In addition to Emily in Paris, Gouery is best known for The White Lotus, where he played Didier. He also starred in the 2024 film Modi: Three Days on the Wing of Madness, though he admitted to feeling hesitant about working with the film’s controversial director, Johnny Depp, at first. “After the trial [with his ex-wife Amber Heard, I felt] that it was OK. I cannot pretend to know whether [someone is] guilty or not guilty – the trial said it. I have to trust the justice,” he told Rolling Stone.
Some other works you may have seen him in include French shows Doc Martin (2011-2015) and President Alphonse (2017), and Italian film Rose Island (2020).

data.title: Duopoly of Air India, IndiGo to face competition from 2 new airlines
data.description:
India’s aviation sector is poised to become more competitive after authorities granted initial approval to two new airlines to start operations in the country, in a move that will further challenge the duopoly of IndiGo and Air India.
The expansion comes as the sector was rattled by a series of setbacks this year, including IndiGo’s massive flight cancellations earlier this month and the crash of an Air India plane in Ahmedabad in June.
On Tuesday, Civil Aviation Minister Kinjarapu Ram Mohan Naidu said in a social media post that his ministry had issued a “no-objection certificate” to Al Hind Air and FlyExpress this week in a bid to encourage ‍more competition in the domestic market.
According to Al Hind’s website, the airline plans to begin operations in southern India with a fleet ‌of ATR Turboprop aircraft and is in the process of obtaining an air operator certificate.
FlyExpress did not give any details about its launch, with a banner on its website indicating “coming soon”.
The entry of the two new airlines is expected to pressure IndiGo and Air India to raise their competitiveness after several major setbacks.

During the current holiday season, IndiGo cancelled about 4,500 flights due to staff shortage following new policies introduced by the country’s aviation authorities to increase rest time for pilots. The cancellations left hundreds of thousands of passengers stranded and scrambling to find alternative travel arrangements.
Air India’s Boeing 787 Dreamliner crash in June, which killed 260 people, forced the airline to suspend 83 flights for six weeks to complete government-mandated safety checks on its fleet.
“After the IndiGo crisis, the government has realised that the monopoly of two airlines needs to be broken. They are going to be giving more licences. It is a very good opportunity for people who want to invest in the sector,” said Subhash Goyal, chairman of the aviation and tourism committee at the Indian Chamber of Commerce.
IndiGo and Air India control more than 90 per cent of India’s aviation market, with smaller competitors such as SpiceJet and Akasa accounting for the rest. The two dominant airlines have embarked on massive fleet expansion programmes, each ordering hundreds of new Airbus and Boeing jets.
Since India liberalised its aviation sector in 1990, major carriers such as Air India and smaller players have struggled to cope with intense competition and high operating costs. Kingfisher Airlines and Jet Airways were among the new entrants after the policy change, but they ceased operations in 2012 and 2019, respectively.
As its losses continued to mount, state-owned Air India became privately owned after it was bought over by the Tata group in 2022.

Since 2020, India has granted operational permits to six airlines, including several regional carriers.
Goyal said taxes on aviation turbine fuel, as well as landing and parking charges, were steep and called for these major operational expenses to be cut to improve the sector’s competitiveness.
He also urged New Delhi to provide financial and other support to India-based airlines, citing the strong state support for Singapore Airlines and Air France.
Onus on authorities
The impending launch of Al Hind Air and FlyExpress comes as India seeks to make flying more affordable in what some analysts say is the world’s fastest-growing aviation market.
The government plans to build more airports to support the sector’s growth.
A second airport in Mumbai, the Navi Mumbai International Airport, which opened on Thursday, is expected to reduce air traffic congestion in India’s financial centre.

Julian Fantauzzo, the founder of European Aviation Consulting, said the second airport in Mumbai would only serve the market’s growth if operations between the city’s two airports were seamlessly integrated, such as for ground transport links and cargo operations.
Delhi is also set to welcome a second airport at Jewar next month, with the first phase focusing on domestic flights before the rollout of international services.
The transformation of India’s aviation sector, however, also requires policymakers to strengthen the aviation regulator so that it can improve the reliability and competitiveness of airlines and airports, according to Fantazzo.
He called for an increase in the regulator’s headcount to deal with major hiccups, such as the IndiGo flight cancellations, and its enforcement powers to carry out independent audits.

data.title: Aspiring Bangladesh PM Tarique Rahman welcomed home after 17 years abroad
data.description:
Aspiring prime minister and political heavyweight Tarique Rahman was welcomed back to Bangladesh on Thursday by huge crowds of joyous supporters after 17 years in self-imposed exile.
Rahman, the son of former prime minister Khaleda Zia and heir apparent of the Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP), shook hands with party leaders after touching down at the Dhaka airport.
Accompanied by his wife and daughter, a visibly emotional Rahman took off his shoes, stood on the grass outside the airport and scooped up the soil as a mark of reverence.
He waved to supporters before ducking into a convoy under tight security, video posted by his party showed.
Since early Thursday, BNP backers started gathering in the capital, plastering the streets with banners and festoons bearing images of Rahman.

Patriotic songs blared from loudspeakers while cut-outs depicted the 60-year-old politician riding a stallion.
Alamgir Hossain, a BNP supporter, said the country was in a “dire situation” and that only Rahman “can fix it”.
Rahman left Bangladesh for London in 2008 after what he has described as political persecution.
As acting chairman of the BNP, Rahman will lead the party through the February 12 general election, the first polls since ex-premier Sheikh Hasina’s exit following a student-led uprising last year.

The BNP is widely seen as an election front runner, with Rahman expected to be put forward as prime minister if his party wins a majority.
Rahman’s ailing mother, 80-year-old former leader Zia, is undergoing treatment at a hospital in Dhaka.
Despite years of ill health and imprisonment, Zia vowed in November to campaign in the upcoming elections.
But she was hospitalised soon after making that pledge and has been in intensive care ever since.
Rahman’s return comes after recent unrest over the killing of popular student leader Sharif Osman Hadi, a staunch India critic who took part in last year’s mass uprising.


Hadi, 32, was shot by masked assailants this month in Dhaka, and later died of his wounds at a Singapore hospital.
His death set off violent protests, with mobs torching several buildings, including two major newspapers deemed to favour India, as well as a prominent cultural institution.
Mobs also pelted stones at the Indian High Commission in the port city of Chattogram, where India has since suspended visa services.
Dhaka’s diplomatic ties have worsened with its historical ally India, where Hasina has sought refuge since the uprising that ended her 15 year autocratic rule.
India says it is still considering Dhaka’s requests to extradite Hasina, who was sentenced to death in absentia for orchestrating a deadly crackdown on the uprising.

Anti-India sentiments were stirred afresh in the majority Muslim nation after a Hindu garment worker was accused of blasphemy and lynched by a mob on December 18.
Jahan Panna, a former BNP lawmaker, said she hoped Rahman’s return would end the “cycle of anarchy” prevailing in the country.
“Tarique Rahman is the symbol of hope for this country,” said Panna, 55.
Rahman faced a slew of criminal cases but since Hasina’s fall from power, he has been acquitted of the most serious charge against him: a life sentence handed down in absentia for a 2004 grenade attack on a political rally. He denied the charges.
In Britain, he kept a low profile but remained an outspoken figure on social media.
In June, he met in London with Muhammad Yunus, the 85-year-old Nobel Peace Prize winner leading the interim government until the February elections.
Hasina’s Awami League party, a bitter rival of the BNP, has been barred from contesting in the polls.

data.title: Indonesians in flood-struck Sumatra hold Christmas mass
data.description:
At a church in Sumatra, dozens of worshippers sang hymns at a Christmas mass, gathered together for their first service since deadly floods swept the Indonesian island.
The Angkola Protestant Church, in the hard-hit South Tapanuli district, was festooned on Wednesday with balloons and simple Christmas decorations.
Outside, the street leading to the building was buried under mounds of debris and foliage.
Many in the congregation are still sheltering at evacuation sites after the disaster wreaked havoc on the island four weeks ago.
Church-goer Krismanto Nainggolan said this year’s Christmas service was “different”, even as he noted joy in the bittersweet moment.


“The feelings are mixed. Every word of the pastor’s sermon made us want to cry,” he said after the Christmas mass.
“But the spirit of Christmas … gave us strength,” he added.
Krismanto lost his house in the flooding, while many of his neighbours were killed.
According to the National Disaster Mitigation Agency, 1,129 people died and more than 170 others are still missing.

While the annual monsoon season often brings heavy rain to Indonesia, this month’s deluge was among the worst disasters to strike Sumatra since a magnitude 9.1 earthquake triggered a massive tsunami in 2004.
In South Tapanuli, church-goer Mea Rosmawati Zebua said she had not expected to be able to celebrate Christmas this year.
“In past years, Christmas was a routine. Now, (we are) very grateful because God still gives us the breath of life,” the 54-year-old said.
While Christmas mass is typically held in the evening, the Angkola church moved its service to Wednesday afternoon ahead of rain forecast in the evening, Pastor Yansen Roberto Ritonga said.
To prepare for the first service since the disaster, the church had to remove towering heaps of mud that had been washed inside.

Soldiers and police had helped clear the debris and driftwood.
On Wednesday afternoon, a man rang the church’s bell before the pastor’s entrance, marking the start of the mass.
Around 30 worshippers, each of them holding a lit candle, sang Christmas hymns.
Yansen said this year’s Christmas served as a moment of “reflection” for the congregation.
Church-goer Krismanto said that despite the widespread damage and the personal cost of the disaster, he chose to see it as a new beginning.
“Our hopes depend solely on God because we are now starting over … our lives are starting anew,” he said.

data.title: From Shein to Labubu, French shoppers are buying Chinese for Christmas
data.description:
The first physical store of Chinese-owned fast-fashion giant Shein – which opened in central Paris in November amid heated protests – has become a new retail destination for many denizens of the French capital as they rush to finish their Christmas shopping.
Hundreds of customers wandered among the racks of clothing, rifling through items carrying tags with rock-bottom prices, including a €66.99 (US$79) leather jacket and a €78.99 puffer coat.
Those wishing to use the fitting rooms at the Shein shop – which takes up almost half the top floor of Paris’ renowned BHV department store – had a long wait. Near the snaking queue, one saleswoman addressed a customer’s complaints of insufficient supplies.
“See the construction over there? We are planning to expand the store,” she said. “It will be bigger, and we will have a lot more things in stock.”
Seemingly unaffected by the complaints which greeted its opening, levied by the French fashion industry as well as some politicians and civil groups, the Shein store in Paris appears well entrenched for the Christmas season.
Meanwhile, Labubu, the wildly popular doll from Chinese toy company Pop Mart, remains a top entry on holiday shopping lists. Retail guides from major French newspapers, including Le Parisien and Ouest-France, have included instructions for how readers can find the fluffy, elusive figurines.
After a series of videos on the short video platform TikTok – owned by Chinese tech company ByteDance – many are using the dolls as decorations for their Christmas tree.


Parisian Gaspard Bernard joined in on the trend, festooning his home with Labubus after his boyfriend saw videos of other couples doing the same.
“I started decorating my tree,” he said. “I wanted to go for champagne colours … then he got here and put Labubus everywhere,” Bernard added with a smile.
Ultimately, the tree was adorned with traditional Christmas symbols and seven multicoloured Labubus, which Bernard said received mixed reviews from his friends.
The doll has been the subject of other humorous videos on the platform, with one featuring a forlorn user saying he would prefer a limited edition Labubu over what he got for Christmas: a multicooker.
Pop Mart did not miss out on the promotional opportunity generated by these and similar videos. Along with its regular holiday marketing, the company published a French-language article in November making the case for a Labubu as an ideal Christmas gift.
Not all are approaching the frenzy with benign intent. Counterfeiters have attempted to take advantage of supply shortages to sell ersatz dolls, known colloquially as “Lafufus”, at a high mark-up.
French authorities have responded in kind, with police in the southern town of Clermont-Ferrand launching an operation at the end of November to curb the trade of knock-off dolls and urging residents to check items carefully while doing their Christmas shopping.
